brief: GAP/WHY: Cardioneuroablation (CNA) targets ganglionated plexi (GPs) to modulate cardiac autonomic tone in functional AV block and cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope. Optimal mapping strategy to localize GP/autonomic targets is undefined; unclear how fractionated-area mapping vs peak-frequency-area mapping compare, and how peak frequency varies regionally across cardiac segments. DESIGN/SETTING: Single-center retrospective analysis; high-density electroanatomical mapping (EAM) during CNA. POPULATION/N: 10 patients undergoing CNA - cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope n=7, functional AV block n=3. METHODS/COMPARISON: Two mapping approaches compared - fractionated area mapping vs peak frequency area mapping (threshold >550 Hz). Both assessed across 22 predefined cardiac regions. KEY RESULTS (exact numbers): (1) Median fractionated area = 0.8 cm2 (IQR 0.3-1.6 cm2). (2) Median area of regions with peak frequency >550 Hz = 0.6 cm2 (IQR 0.3-1.0 cm2); fractionated vs peak-freq area p=0.152 (no significant difference). (3) Median peak frequency at left superior GP where asystole/bradycardia responses occurred = 623.0 Hz (IQR 582.0-668.0 Hz). (4) Median peak frequency in regions with tachycardia responses = 602.0 Hz (IQR 582.0-668.0 Hz); bradycardia/asystole vs tachycardia p=0.738 (no significant difference). (5) Ablation sites overlapped BOTH mapping areas in 7 of 10 patients; 2 patients had ablation sites limited to peak-frequency areas only. (6) Peak frequency values highest in septal and posterior atrial walls, superior vena cava (SVC), and GP regions - indicating heightened autonomic activity in those zones. TAKEAWAY: Fractionated-area mapping and peak-frequency (>550 Hz) mapping give complementary (not interchangeable) information on CNA targets; regional peak-frequency variation reflects autonomic heterogeneity. Supports integrating multiple mapping modalities for procedural precision and better outcomes.
